name: Garter Stitch
description: The most basic of all knitting stitch patterns, created by knitting every row when working flat. Produces a flat, reversible, ridged fabric with horizontal ridges on both sides. Lies flat and does not curl at edges.
instructions:
Cast on any number of stitches.
Row 1 (RS): Knit all stitches.
Row 2 (WS): Knit all stitches.
Repeat Rows 1-2 for desired length.
Bind off loosely.

When knitting in the round, alternate knit one round, purl one round.

stitch_key.note: Any number of stitches. 1-row repeat (knit every row when working flat).

notes: Often used as a non-curling border for stockinette. Also called 'plain knitting.'

name: Stockinette Stitch
description: The classic smooth V-patterned fabric most people associate with knitting. Created by alternating knit rows with purl rows when working flat. Tends to curl at edges — add a garter or seed stitch border.
instructions:
Cast on any number of stitches.
Row 1 (RS): Knit all stitches.
Row 2 (WS): Purl all stitches.
Repeat Rows 1-2 for desired length.
Bind off.

In the round: knit every round (no purling needed).

stitch_key.note: Any number of stitches. Row 1 knit, Row 2 purl. In the round: knit every round.

notes: Abbreviated 'St st' in patterns. The most commonly used stitch in knitting.

name: Seed Stitch
description: A textured reversible stitch pattern where single knits and purls alternate both horizontally and vertically, creating small bumps that resemble scattered seeds. Lies flat with no curling.
instructions:
Cast on an even number of stitches.
Row 1 (RS): *K1, p1; repeat from * to end.
Row 2 (WS): *P1, k1; repeat from * to end.
Repeat Rows 1-2 for desired length.
Bind off in pattern.

For odd number of stitches:
Row 1 (RS): *K1, p1; repeat from * to last st, k1.
Row 2 (WS): *P1, k1; repeat from * to last st, p1.

stitch_key.note: Multiple of 2 stitches. 2-row repeat. Also works with odd number.

notes: Called 'moss stitch' in UK terminology. Remember: knit the purls, purl the knits.

name: Moss Stitch (American)
description: A textured reversible stitch worked on a 4-row repeat. Two rows of *k1, p1* are followed by two rows of *p1, k1*, creating stacked knit and purl bumps that shift every two rows. More pronounced texture than seed stitch.
instructions:
Cast on an even number of stitches.
Row 1 (RS): *K1, p1; repeat from * to end.
Row 2 (WS): *K1, p1; repeat from * to end.
Row 3: *P1, k1; repeat from * to end.
Row 4: *P1, k1; repeat from * to end.
Repeat Rows 1-4 for desired length.
Bind off in pattern.

stitch_key.note: Multiple of 2 stitches. 4-row repeat: 2 rows k1,p1 then 2 rows p1,k1.

notes: Not to be confused with UK moss stitch (which is US seed stitch). Also called Double Moss or Irish Moss.

name: 1x1 Ribbing
description: A stretchy, elastic stitch pattern created by alternating single knit and purl stitches. The knit stitches form vertical columns (ribs) that stand out. Ideal for cuffs, hems, neckbands, and sock tops.
instructions:
Cast on an even number of stitches.
Row 1 (RS and all rows): *K1, p1; repeat from * to end.
Repeat Row 1 for desired length.
Bind off in pattern using a stretchy bind-off.

For odd number of stitches:
Row 1: *K1, p1; repeat from * to last st, k1.
Row 2: *P1, k1; repeat from * to last st, p1.
Repeat Rows 1-2.

stitch_key.note: Multiple of 2 stitches. 1-row repeat. With odd stitches, use 2-row repeat.

notes: Use a stretchy cast-on. 'Knit the knits, purl the purls.'

name: 2x2 Ribbing
description: A stretchier and more pronounced ribbing pattern than 1x1, created by alternating two knit stitches with two purl stitches. Commonly used for sweater cuffs, hems, hats, and blanket edges.
instructions:
Cast on a multiple of 4 stitches.
Row 1 (RS and all rows): *K2, p2; repeat from * to end.
Repeat Row 1 for desired length.
Bind off in pattern using a stretchy bind-off.

For symmetrical edges (multiples of 4 + 2):
Row 1 (RS): *K2, p2; repeat from * to last 2 sts, k2.
Row 2 (WS): *P2, k2; repeat from * to last 2 sts, p2.

stitch_key.note: Multiple of 4 stitches. 1-row repeat. For symmetrical edges, use multiples of 4 + 2.

notes: For hats in the round, cast on multiples of 4. Stretchy cast-on recommended.

name: Basketweave Stitch
description: A classic textured stitch that creates a woven basket-like fabric using blocks of knit and purl stitches. In the 4x4 version, 4 knit stitches alternate with 4 purl stitches for 4 rows, then the pattern reverses.
instructions:
Cast on a multiple of 8 stitches.
Rows 1-4: *K4, p4; repeat from * to end.
Rows 5-8: *P4, k4; repeat from * to end.
Repeat Rows 1-8 for desired length.
Bind off in pattern.

Centered version (multiples of 8 + 6):
Rows 1 & 3: K1, *p4, k4; repeat from * to last 5 sts, p4, k1.
Rows 2 & 4: P1, *k4, p4; repeat from * to last 5 sts, k4, p1.
Rows 5 & 7: P1, *k4, p4; repeat from * to last 5 sts, k4, p1.
Rows 6 & 8: K1, *p4, k4; repeat from * to last 5 sts, p4, k1.

stitch_key.note: Multiple of 8 stitches. 8-row repeat: 4 rows k4,p4 then 4 rows p4,k4.

notes: Customizable block size: 3x3, 5x5, etc. Knit loosely as this pattern tightens up.

name: Diagonal Basket Weave
description: A dense, woven-looking stitch created by crossing stitches over one another in a 2-row repeat, similar to tiny 1-stitch cables. Produces a thick fabric with diagonal crossed lines.
instructions:
Cast on an odd number of stitches.
Row 1 (RS): Skip first st, knit the 2nd st through back loop (do not drop), knit the 1st st normally, drop both; repeat across to last st, knit last st.
Row 2 (WS): Skip first st, purl the 2nd st (do not drop), purl the 1st st, drop both; repeat across to last st, knit last st.
Repeat Rows 1-2 for desired length.
Bind off loosely.

stitch_key.note: Odd number of stitches. 2-row repeat. Stitches worked out of order for crossed diagonal effect.

notes: Use needles 1-2 sizes larger than recommended. Fabric may curl — add a border.

name: Feather and Fan (Old Shale)
description: A classic Shetland lace pattern that creates beautiful scalloped, wave-like edges. Despite its intricate appearance, it uses only a 4-row repeat with grouped decreases and yarn overs. Popular for scarves, shawls, and baby blankets.
instructions:
Cast on a multiple of 18 stitches.
Row 1 (RS): Knit all stitches.
Row 2 (WS): Purl all stitches.
Row 3: *(K2tog) 3 times, (yo, k1) 6 times, (k2tog) 3 times; repeat from * to end.
Row 4: Knit all stitches.
Repeat Rows 1-4 for desired length.
Bind off loosely.

stitch_key.note: Multiple of 18 stitches. 4-row repeat. Row 3 is the pattern row.

notes: Also called Old Shale. Blocking opens up the lace pattern significantly.

name: Cable Twist
description: A classic 6-stitch cable that twists every 6th row, creating a rope-like column. The cable cross is worked using a cable needle, holding stitches in front or back to create the twist direction.
instructions:
Cast on a multiple of 12 stitches (6-st cable + 6-st purl background).
Rows 1, 3, 5 (RS): P6, *k6, p6; repeat from *.
Row 2 (WS): K6, *p6, k6; repeat from *.
Row 4 (Cable Cross RS): P6, *slip 3 to CN hold back, k3, k3 from CN, p6; repeat from *.
Row 6 (WS): Repeat Row 2.
Repeat Rows 1-6 for desired length.
Bind off.

stitch_key.note: Multiple of 12 stitches. 6-row repeat with cable cross on Row 4.

notes: Use a cable needle. For a left-leaning cable, hold stitches in front. For right-leaning, hold in back.

name: Single Crochet
description: The most basic and fundamental crochet stitch. Creates a dense, tight, sturdy fabric with minimal stretch. All other crochet stitches are variations of this stitch. The shortest standard crochet stitch (1 chain in height).
instructions:
Foundation: Chain any number (e.g., ch 11 for 10 sc).
Row 1: Insert hook into 2nd ch from hook. Yarn over, draw through chain (2 loops on hook). Yarn over, draw through both loops. Repeat in each ch across. Turn.
Row 2: Ch 1 (turning chain, does NOT count as stitch). Insert hook under top 2 loops of first sc. Yarn over, draw up loop (2 loops). Yarn over, draw through both loops. Repeat across. Turn.
Repeat Row 2.

stitch_key.note: Single row repeat. Ch 1 turning chain does not count as stitch.

notes: US terminology. Ch 1 turning chain does NOT count as a stitch.

name: Double Crochet
description: A foundational stitch twice as tall as single crochet. Creates a lighter, more flexible fabric with small gaps. The workhorse of crochet — the default for blankets, garments, scarves, and granny squares.
instructions:
Foundation: Chain any number plus 2 extra (e.g., ch 12 for 10 dc).
Row 1: Yarn over, insert hook into 4th ch from hook (3 skipped ch count as first dc). Yarn over, draw through ch (3 loops). Yarn over, draw through first 2 loops (2 loops). Yarn over, draw through last 2 loops. Repeat across. Turn.
Row 2: Ch 3 (counts as first dc). Yarn over, insert hook into next stitch. Work dc. Repeat across. Work last dc into top of turning ch-3. Turn.
Repeat Row 2.

stitch_key.note: Single row repeat. Ch 3 turning chain counts as first stitch.

notes: US terminology. Ch 3 turning chain COUNTED as first dc.

name: Half Double Crochet
description: A medium-height stitch between single crochet and double crochet. Creates a fabric slightly looser than sc but more dense than dc. Versatile and commonly used for hats, scarves, sweaters, and baby blankets.
instructions:
Foundation: Chain any number plus 1 extra (e.g., ch 11 for 10 hdc).
Row 1: Yarn over, insert hook into 3rd ch from hook. Yarn over, draw through ch (3 loops). Yarn over, draw through all 3 loops. Repeat across. Turn.
Row 2: Ch 2 (counts as first hdc). Yarn over, skip first stitch, insert hook into next stitch. Draw up loop (3 loops). Yarn over, draw through all 3 loops. Repeat across. Work last hdc into top of turning ch-2. Turn.
Repeat Row 2.

stitch_key.note: Single row repeat. Ch 2 turning chain counts as first stitch.

notes: US terminology. Ch 2 turning chain counts as first stitch.

name: Granny Stitch
description: A classic textured pattern built from clusters of 3 double crochet worked into the spaces between clusters. Creates a dense, textured fabric. The stitch that defines the iconic granny square, but can be worked in rows.
instructions:
Foundation: Chain a multiple of 3 (e.g., ch 15).
Row 1: Work 2 dc in 3rd ch from hook. *Skip 2 ch, 3 dc in next ch. Repeat from * across. Turn.
Row 2: Ch 2 (counts as first dc). Work 3 dc in first space between clusters. *Work 3 dc in next space. Repeat from * across. Work 1 dc in top of turning ch. Turn.
Row 3: Ch 2, 2 dc in first space. *Work 3 dc in each space across. Turn.
Repeat Rows 2-3.

stitch_key.note: Multiple of 3. 2-row repeat. Clusters of 3 dc worked into spaces, not stitches.

notes: US terminology. Clusters are worked into spaces between clusters, not into stitch tops.

name: Shell Stitch
description: A classic stitch creating beautiful scalloped, fan-shaped patterns by working 5 double crochet into the same stitch. Shells are anchored by single crochet stitches. Produces an open, lacy fabric with excellent drape.
instructions:
Foundation: Chain a multiple of 6 + 2 (e.g., ch 14).
Row 1: Sc in 2nd ch from hook. *Skip 2 ch, 5 dc in next ch (shell made), skip 2 ch, sc in next ch. Repeat from * across. Turn.
Row 2: Ch 3 (counts as dc), 2 dc in first sc (half shell). *Skip 2 dc, sc in next dc (center of shell), skip 2 dc, 5 dc in next sc (full shell). Repeat from * ending with 3 dc in last sc. Turn.
Row 3: Ch 1, sc in first dc. *Skip 2 dc, 5 dc in next sc, skip 2 dc, sc in next dc (center of shell). Repeat from * ending with sc in top of ch-3. Turn.
Repeat Rows 2-3.

stitch_key.note: Multiple of 6 + 2. 2-row repeat. Shell = 5 dc in same stitch. Half shell = 3 dc at edges.

notes: US terminology.

name: V-Stitch
description: A beautiful, open lace pattern created by working (dc, ch 1, dc) into the same stitch, forming a 'V' shape. Airy, lightweight, and works up quickly. Perfect for summer tops, shawls, and lightweight blankets.
instructions:
Foundation: Chain a multiple of 3 + 1 (e.g., ch 22).
Row 1: Dc in 5th ch from hook. Ch 1, dc in same ch (first V-stitch made). *Skip 2 ch, (dc, ch 1, dc) in next ch. Repeat from * across. Ch 3, turn.
Row 2: (Dc, ch 1, dc) in first ch-1 space. *Skip next dc, (dc, ch 1, dc) in next ch-1 space. Repeat from * across. Dc in top of turning ch-3. Ch 3, turn.
Repeat Row 2.

stitch_key.note: Multiple of 3 + 1. Single row repeat. V-stitch = (dc, ch 1, dc) in same st or space.

notes: US terminology. Work into ch-1 spaces of previous row.

name: Waffle Stitch
description: Creates a beautiful, dimensional grid pattern resembling a waffle. Combines regular double crochet with front post double crochet (FPdc) to create raised 'walls' and recessed 'pockets'. Thick, warm, and highly textured.
instructions:
Foundation: Chain a multiple of 3 + 2 (e.g., ch 17 for 15 sts).
Special stitch: Front Post Double Crochet (FPdc) — yo, insert hook from front to back to front around the post of the stitch below, complete as regular dc.

Row 1 (WS): Dc in 3rd ch from hook and in each ch across. Turn.
Row 2 (RS): Ch 2. *FPdc in next st, dc in next 2 sts. Repeat from * to last 2 sts. FPdc, dc. Turn.
Row 3: Ch 2. *Dc in next st, FPdc in next 2 sts. Repeat from * to last 2 sts. Dc in each of last 2 sts. Turn.
Repeat Rows 2-3.

stitch_key.note: Multiple of 3. 2-row repeat using FPdc. RS row: FPdc-dc-dc. WS row: dc-FPdc-FPdc.

notes: US terminology. Uses front post double crochet (FPdc).

name: Bobble Stitch
description: Creates raised, rounded bumps on the fabric surface by working multiple incomplete double crochet into the same stitch and closing together. Bobbles pop to the right side for a highly textured, dimensional fabric.
instructions:
Foundation: Chain a multiple of 4 + 3 (e.g., ch 23).
Special stitch — Bobble (bo): Yarn over, insert hook, pull up loop, pull through 2 loops. Repeat 4 more times in same st (6 loops on hook). Yarn over, pull through all 6 loops.

Base Row: Sc in 3rd ch from hook, sc across. Turn.
Row 1 (WS): Ch 2 (hdc). *Bobble in next st, sc, hdc in next 2 sts. Repeat from * to last st. Hdc. Turn.
Row 2: Ch 2, hdc across. Turn.
Row 3: Ch 2, hdc in next 2 sts. *Bobble, sc, hdc in next 2 sts. Repeat from *. Bobble, sc, hdc. Turn.
Row 4: Repeat Row 2.
Repeat Rows 1-4.

stitch_key.note: Multiple of 4 + 3. 4-row repeat. Bobble = 5 incomplete dc closed together.

notes: US terminology. Worked from WS so bobbles pop to RS.

name: Corner to Corner (C2C)
description: A unique technique worked diagonally from one corner to the opposite corner using small 'tiles' (ch-3 + 3 dc). First half increases tiles per row until widest point, then decreases to the opposite corner. Ideal for graph-based colorwork designs.
instructions:
Increase Phase:
Row 1: Ch 6. Dc in 4th ch from hook, dc in next 2 ch. (1 tile)
Row 2: Ch 6, dc in 4th ch from hook, dc in next 2 ch. Sl st into ch-3 sp of previous tile. Ch 3, 3 dc in same sp. Turn. (2 tiles)
Row 3: Ch 6, dc in 4th ch from hook, dc in next 2 ch. *Sl st into next ch-3 sp, ch 3, 3 dc in same sp. Repeat from * across. Turn.
Repeat Row 3, increasing 1 tile per row, until desired width.

Decrease Phase:
Sl st in first 3 dc and into ch-3 sp (eliminates first tile). Ch 3, 3 dc in same sp. *Sl st into next ch-3 sp, ch 3, 3 dc in same sp. Repeat from * across. Do NOT work tile in last sp. Turn.
Repeat Decrease Row until 1 tile remains. Fasten off.

stitch_key.note: No fixed multiple — grows diagonally. 1 tile = ch 3 + 3 dc.

notes: US terminology. Mini C2C uses hdc for smaller tiles.

name: Moss Stitch (Crochet)
description: Also known as granite stitch, linen stitch, or woven stitch. Alternates single crochet and chain stitches, with next row's sc worked into chain spaces. Creates a firm, stable fabric with a beautiful woven texture that lies flat naturally.
instructions:
Foundation: Chain an even number (e.g., ch 16).
Row 1: Sc in 4th ch from hook. *Ch 1, skip 1 ch, sc in next ch. Repeat from * across. Ch 2, turn.
Row 2: Sc in first ch-1 space (not in first sc). *Ch 1, sc in next ch-1 space. Repeat from * across. Work final sc in space beside turning ch. Ch 2, turn.
Repeat Row 2.

stitch_key.note: Multiple of 2 (even number). Single row repeat. All sc worked into ch-1 spaces.

notes: US terminology. Also called linen stitch or granite stitch.
